modules.title: Article writing
modules.html:
An article is written for a newspaper or magazine and read by the general public. It must have a catchy title, a clear by-line, and an engaging opening.

Structure

  1. Title — short, striking (e.g. Why Every Youth Must Vote).
  2. By-line — By Amaka Okoye.
  3. Introduction — hook the reader and state the issue.
  4. Body — 3–5 well-developed paragraphs with subheadings if useful.
  5. Conclusion — restate the point / call to action.

Tone: informative, slightly conversational but not slangy.

examples.q: Give a possible title for an article on the dangers of drug abuse among youths.
examples.a: e.g. Silent Killer: The Rising Menace of Drug Abuse Among Youths.

modules.title: Speeches and formal reports
modules.html:
A speech is meant to be spoken. It has:

  1. Salutation — The Principal, distinguished guests, teachers, fellow students…
  2. Opening — greet, state occasion, state your topic.
  3. Body — organised points, examples, personal touches.
  4. Closing — memorable line + Thank you for listening.

Use short sentences, repetition and rhetorical questions to engage listeners.

Formal report

  • Title / heading — subject + author + date.
  • Introduction — purpose and scope.
  • Findings — under sub-headings or numbered points.
  • Conclusion — summary of findings.
  • Recommendations — what should be done next.

examples.q: Suggest a strong opening line for a speech on the importance of reading.
examples.a: e.g. A room without books, said Cicero, is a body without a soul — and my aim today is to argue why every one of us must feed our souls with words.

modules.title: Minutes of meeting and business letters
modules.html:
Minutes record proceedings of a meeting.

  1. Heading: Minutes of the meeting of ... held on ...
  2. List of members present, absent with / without apology.
  3. Adoption of the previous minutes.
  4. Matters arising.
  5. Agenda items — numbered, each with a heading and a summary of decisions.
  6. Any other business (AOB).
  7. Date and time of next meeting.
  8. Adjournment.
  9. Signature of secretary and chairperson.

Business letters (a formal letter for commercial purposes) include: writer's address, date, recipient's address, salutation, subject line, body (purpose → details → action), complimentary close, signature and typed name/title.

examples.q: Which section of the minutes lists items to be discussed?
examples.a: The agenda — usually included in the opening notice; discussed as numbered items in the minutes.
